It formalized the 'effective occupation' principle and accelerated the Scramble for Africa—by 1914, 90% of Africa was under European colonial rule. AP Period 6 tests it under GOV and SIO as the moment European powers institutionalized African partition, with lasting consequences for 20th-century state boundaries and ethnic conflicts.

questions.prompt: Imperial resource practice associated with coercive labor and environmental exploitation.
questions.explanation: King Leopold II's Congo Free State (1885–1908) used forced labor quotas to harvest wild rubber—enforced by severing the hands of those who missed quotas. An international human rights campaign exposed the atrocities. Amazon rubber barons used similar coercion. AP Period 6 tests rubber extraction under ECN and SIO as an extreme case of imperial resource extraction creating humanitarian catastrophe—and as an early example of international human rights activism.

questions.prompt: Canal linking the Mediterranean and Red Seas, increasing European strategic interest in Egypt.
questions.explanation: The Suez Canal (opened 1869), built with Egyptian labor under khedive Ismail with French engineering, cut the Europe–Asia shipping route by ~7,000 miles. Britain bought Egypt's shares in 1875 and occupied Egypt in 1882 to secure the canal. It became a symbol of imperial strategic control. AP Period 6 tests the Suez Canal under ECN and GOV as infrastructure whose strategic value drove formal British imperialism in Egypt—a classic case of economic interest becoming territorial control.

questions.prompt: Global conflict from 1914 to 1918.
questions.explanation: World War I (1914–1918) killed ~20 million and wounded ~21 million, drawing in European colonial empires and making it a truly global conflict with fighting in Africa, the Middle East, and Asia-Pacific. Industrialized warfare—machine guns, poison gas, artillery—produced unprecedented stalemate on the Western Front. AP Period 7 tests WWI under GOV and TEC as the event that shattered 19th-century optimism, redrew empires, and set conditions for WWII.

questions.prompt: Militarism, alliances, imperialism, and nationalism as causes of World War I.
questions.explanation: The MAIN framework identifies four structural causes of WWI: Militarism (arms race, glorification of war), Alliances (Triple Entente vs. Triple Alliance creating chain reactions), Imperialism (colonial rivalry generating tensions), and Nationalism (ethnic self-determination destabilizing Austria-Hungary and the Balkans). The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand (June 28, 1914) triggered these structural conditions. AP Period 7 uses MAIN as an analytical lens for causation.

questions.prompt: Glorification and buildup of military power before World War I.
questions.explanation: Pre-WWI militarism involved rapidly expanding standing armies and navies—Germany doubled its army 1900–1914; the Anglo-German naval race produced dreadnought fleets—alongside a culture glorifying war as ennobling. Military planners (Schlieffen Plan) expected short, decisive wars. AP Period 7 tests militarism under GOV and TEC as a structural cause of WWI and a symptom of intensified European nationalism and imperial competition.

questions.prompt: This kind of war mobilizes civilians, factories, food, propaganda, and the whole economy, not just soldiers.
questions.explanation: Total war mobilized entire national economies and populations for the war effort—factories converted to munitions, women entered the workforce, food rationed, dissent censored. WWI was the first industrial total war; WWII intensified it further. AP Period 7 tests total war under GOV, TEC, and SIO as a concept explaining how industrial-age war erased the distinction between combatants and civilians and expanded state power over society.

questions.prompt: Civilian production, rationing, propaganda, and labor organization to support war.
questions.explanation: Home front mobilization in WWI and WWII converted civilian economies to war production, rationed consumer goods, used propaganda to maintain morale, and recruited women into industry. Women's wartime labor contributed to post-war suffrage movements in Britain (1918) and the U.S. (1920). AP Period 7 tests home front mobilization under GOV and SIO as evidence of how total war transformed gender roles and state power over civilian life.

questions.prompt: Peace settlement whose punishments helped destabilize interwar Europe.
questions.explanation: The Treaty of Versailles (June 28, 1919) ended WWI by assigning Germany sole war guilt (Article 231), imposing reparations of 132 billion gold marks, stripping colonies and territory, and limiting the German military. The humiliation and economic burden fed Weimar instability and Hitler's rise. AP Period 7 tests Versailles under GOV as a failed peace whose punitive terms created conditions for WWII—the classic 'long-term causes of WWII' AP prompt.

questions.prompt: International organization proposed to prevent future wars.
questions.explanation: The League of Nations (1920–1946), Woodrow Wilson's brainchild from his Fourteen Points, was the first international peacekeeping body—with a council, assembly, and Permanent Court. The U.S. Senate refused to join; the League lacked a standing army and failed to stop Japanese expansion (1931), Italian aggression (1935), and German rearmament. AP Period 7 tests it under GOV as an idealistic but ineffective multilateral system, contrasted with the post-WWII United Nations.

questions.prompt: Principle that nations should be able to choose their own political future.
questions.explanation: Woodrow Wilson's Fourteen Points (January 1918) advocated national self-determination—the right of peoples to choose their own governance—as the basis for the post-WWI order. Applied selectively to European nations (Poland, Czechoslovakia), it was denied to colonized peoples (India, Vietnam, Korea), fueling anti-colonial nationalism. AP tests self-determination under GOV and CDI as a contested principle whose selective application accelerated 20th-century independence movements.

questions.prompt: Post-World War I arrangement placing former Ottoman and German territories under Allied control.
questions.explanation: The League of Nations mandate system (1919) assigned former German and Ottoman territories to Britain and France as 'temporary' trusteeship—Class A mandates (Iraq, Syria, Palestine) deemed 'nearly ready' for independence; Class B and C further from it. In practice it was disguised colonialism. AP Period 7 tests the mandate system under GOV as an example of the gap between Wilsonian self-determination rhetoric and the actual continuation of European imperialism post-WWI.

questions.prompt: Global economic crisis beginning in 1929.
questions.explanation: The Great Depression (1929–1939) began with the Wall Street Crash (October 1929) and spread globally as trade collapsed, banks failed, and unemployment peaked at ~25% in the U.S. and similar levels elsewhere. It destabilized democracies, empowered fascist movements, and prompted economic nationalism. AP Period 7 tests the Great Depression under ECN and GOV as the economic context that made authoritarian political movements in Germany, Italy, and Japan viable.

questions.prompt: Revolution that overthrew the czar and led to communist rule.
questions.explanation: The Russian Revolution occurred in two stages in 1917: the February Revolution overthrew Tsar Nicholas II; the October Revolution brought Lenin's Bolsheviks to power. The Bolsheviks withdrew from WWI (Treaty of Brest-Litovsk), nationalized industry, and fought a brutal civil war (1918–1921). AP Period 7 tests the Russian Revolution under GOV as a radical political transformation producing the world's first communist state and a model for 20th-century revolutionary movements.

questions.prompt: Single-party rule, command economy, censorship, and no opposition parties describe this political form in the USSR and Maoist China.
questions.explanation: Communism as practiced in the 20th century (Marxism-Leninism) combined single-party rule, state ownership of the economy, censorship, and suppression of political opposition, while claiming to advance the interests of the working class. The Soviet Union was its first state incarnation. AP Period 7–8 tests communism under GOV and CDI as one of the three major political ideologies (alongside liberalism and fascism) competing for global dominance in the interwar and Cold War periods.

questions.prompt: Ideology seeking collective ownership and a classless society, often through revolutionary state power.
questions.explanation: War communism (1918–1921) was the Bolshevik emergency economic policy during the Russian Civil War: nationalizing industry, requisitioning grain from peasants, and suppressing private trade. It produced severe famine and economic collapse, leading Lenin to replace it with the New Economic Policy (NEP) in 1921. AP tests war communism under GOV and ECN as an extreme state mobilization policy illustrating the tension between revolutionary ideology and economic reality.

questions.prompt: Authoritarian Soviet system built around one-party rule, central planning, purges, and collectivization.
questions.explanation: Stalin (r. 1924–1953) transformed the Soviet Union through forced collectivization (killing millions in the Ukrainian famine, 1932–33), Five-Year Plans industrializing the economy at massive human cost, and the Great Purge (1936–38) eliminating perceived political rivals. The Stalinist state was a totalitarian system combining state terror with propaganda glorifying the leader. AP tests it under GOV and SIO as a case study in 20th-century authoritarian state-building.

questions.prompt: The system where the state attempts to control political, economic, social, and private life.
questions.explanation: Totalitarianism describes political systems that attempt to control all aspects of public and private life through single-party rule, state censorship, mass propaganda, secret police, and elimination of civil society. Nazi Germany, Stalinist USSR, and Fascist Italy are the canonical examples. AP Period 7 tests it under GOV as a key concept distinguishing 20th-century authoritarian states from earlier autocracies, and as context for both World Wars and the Cold War.

questions.prompt: Let Hitler take the Sudetenland at Munich in 1938 in hopes of avoiding war.
questions.explanation: Appeasement was the British-French policy of accommodating Hitler's territorial demands—culminating in the Munich Agreement (September 1938), which ceded Czechoslovakia's Sudetenland to Germany. Chamberlain declared 'peace for our time'; six months later Germany occupied the rest of Czechoslovakia. AP Period 7 tests appeasement under GOV as a failed conflict-prevention strategy whose failure demonstrated that aggressive totalitarian regimes could not be satisfied by concessions.

questions.prompt: Global conflict from 1939 to 1945.
questions.explanation: World War II (1939–1945) killed ~70–85 million people—the deadliest conflict in human history—involving systematic genocide (Holocaust), strategic bombing of civilians, and the first use of nuclear weapons. It reshaped the global order: decolonization accelerated, the U.S. and USSR became superpowers, and the United Nations replaced the League. AP Period 7 tests WWII under GOV, TEC, and SIO as the defining catastrophe reshaping the post-1945 world.

questions.prompt: Dominance of Japan's military in politics and expansion during the 1930s and World War II.
questions.explanation: Japanese militarism in the 1930s saw the military dominate politics, pursue imperial expansion into Manchuria (1931), China (1937), and Southeast Asia (1941). Ideologically it combined emperor worship, ultranationalism, and Social Darwinist racial hierarchy. Japan's attack on Pearl Harbor (December 7, 1941) brought the U.S. into WWII. AP Period 7 tests Japanese militarism under GOV and CDI as one of three interwar authoritarian expansionist movements alongside German Nazism and Italian Fascism.

questions.prompt: Nuclear weapon used by the United States against Japan in 1945.
questions.explanation: The U.S. dropped atomic bombs on Hiroshima (August 6, 1945, ~140,000 deaths) and Nagasaki (August 9, ~80,000 deaths), hastening Japan's surrender (August 15). The Manhattan Project's success launched the nuclear age and the Cold War arms race. AP Period 7–8 tests the atomic bomb under TEC and GOV—as a turning point in warfare, a factor in Japanese surrender, and as the technology defining Cold War deterrence and the existential threat of mutual assured destruction.

questions.prompt: Limiting goods so supplies could support the war effort.
questions.explanation: Wartime rationing limited civilian consumption of food, fuel, and manufactured goods to direct resources to military production—practiced by all major combatants in WWI and WWII. In the U.S., rationing covered meat, butter, sugar, gasoline, and nylon. AP tests rationing under GOV and SIO as evidence of total war's mobilization of civilian society—and as a vehicle for state control over consumption and gender roles, as women managed household rationing.

questions.prompt: Militaristic, ultra-nationalist, anti-democratic ideology.
questions.explanation: Fascism is a totalitarian ultra-nationalist ideology emphasizing national rebirth, militarism, anti-Marxism, and authoritarian leadership—rejecting both liberal democracy and socialist internationalism. Mussolini's Italy (1922) and Hitler's Germany (1933) are the defining examples. AP Period 7 tests fascism under GOV and CDI as an interwar ideology arising from WWI trauma, economic crisis, and fear of communist revolution—frequently compared to communism in AP essay prompts.

questions.prompt: Totalitarian state under Hitler built on fascism, racism, militarism, and dictatorship.
questions.explanation: Nazi Germany (1933–1945) under Adolf Hitler combined fascist totalitarianism with racial ideology (Aryan supremacy), antisemitism, and expansionist nationalism. The regime used propaganda (Goebbels), the Gestapo, and Nuremberg Laws (1935) to control society. It launched WWII and executed the Holocaust. AP Period 7 tests Nazi Germany under GOV and CDI as the paradigm case of fascist totalitarianism and 20th-century genocide.

questions.prompt: Nazi genocide of Jews and other targeted groups.
questions.explanation: The Holocaust (1941–1945) was the Nazi genocide of approximately 6 million Jews—two-thirds of European Jewry—along with Roma, disabled people, homosexuals, and others, using industrial methods: Auschwitz-Birkenau processed ~1.1 million victims. The Nuremberg Trials (1945–46) established individual criminal responsibility for crimes against humanity. AP Period 7 tests the Holocaust under SIO and GOV as the defining 20th-century genocide and the impetus for post-war human rights frameworks.

questions.prompt: This 1915-1923 Ottoman campaign used deportations, death marches, and massacres against Armenians.
questions.explanation: The Armenian Genocide (1915–1923) saw the Ottoman government under the Young Turks deport and massacre approximately 600,000–1.5 million ethnic Armenians—using death marches, mass shootings, and starvation. It is the 20th century's first genocide. AP Period 7 tests it under GOV and SIO as an example of state-organized ethnic cleansing, nationalist ideology taken to its extreme, and as a precursor to later genocides that the international community failed to prevent.

questions.prompt: State-directed media campaign designed to shape public opinion and mobilize support.
questions.explanation: Mass propaganda—state-controlled media campaigns using posters, film, radio, and rallies—was a defining feature of 20th-century totalitarian states. Goebbels ran Nazi propaganda; Soviet socialist realism glorified Stalin. WWI posters recruited soldiers and dehumanized enemies. AP Period 7 tests mass propaganda under GOV and CDI as a technology-enabled tool of state power, linking TEC (mass media) to political control over public opinion and manufactured consent.

questions.prompt: Ideological rivalry between the United States and Soviet Union.
questions.explanation: The Cold War (c. 1947–1991) was a sustained ideological, political, and proxy-military rivalry between the U.S. (capitalism/liberal democracy) and the USSR (communism), characterized by nuclear deterrence, arms races, space competition, and proxy wars—never direct superpower combat. AP Period 8 tests it under GOV and CDI as the organizing framework of the post-WWII world order, shaping decolonization, regional conflicts, and the global economy.

questions.prompt: Western military alliance formed during the Cold War.
questions.explanation: NATO (North Atlantic Treaty Organization), founded April 4, 1949, committed Western European nations and the U.S. to collective defense—an attack on one member constituted an attack on all (Article 5). It institutionalized U.S. military presence in Europe as a check on Soviet expansion. AP Period 8 tests NATO under GOV as the institutional structure of the Western Cold War bloc, contrasted with the Warsaw Pact as an example of how superpower competition organized global alliances.

questions.prompt: Soviet-led military alliance in Eastern Europe.
questions.explanation: The Warsaw Pact (1955–1991) was the Soviet-led military alliance of Eastern European communist states—established after West Germany joined NATO. Soviet forces used it to suppress the Hungarian Revolution (1956) and Prague Spring (1968). AP tests the Warsaw Pact under GOV as the Eastern Cold War bloc's institutional equivalent to NATO—illustrating how superpower competition organized and controlled client states through formal alliance structures.

questions.prompt: Nuclear standoff between the United States and Soviet Union.
questions.explanation: The Cuban Missile Crisis (October 16–28, 1962) brought the U.S. and USSR to the brink of nuclear war when the U.S. discovered Soviet ballistic missiles in Cuba. A naval blockade and 13 days of secret negotiations produced a resolution: the Soviets withdrew missiles from Cuba; the U.S. pledged not to invade Cuba and secretly removed missiles from Turkey. AP Period 8 tests it under GOV as the Cold War's most dangerous moment, demonstrating both deterrence theory and diplomatic resolution.

questions.prompt: Cold War competition to build more powerful nuclear weapons and delivery systems.
questions.explanation: The nuclear arms race saw the U.S. and USSR rapidly expand their arsenals—the USSR tested its first atomic bomb in 1949, hydrogen bomb in 1953; by 1985 both held ~10,000+ warheads each. Mutual Assured Destruction (MAD) made nuclear war theoretically suicidal for both sides. AP Period 8 tests the arms race under TEC and GOV as both a driver of Cold War tension and the basis for the deterrence logic that (arguably) prevented direct superpower war.

questions.prompt: The 1947 division of British India into India and Pakistan caused mass migration, communal violence, and the Kashmir dispute.
questions.explanation: The 1947 Partition divided British India into Hindu-majority India and Muslim-majority Pakistan, accompanied by the largest mass migration in history—~14 million displaced—and communal violence killing ~200,000–2 million people. It was accelerated by British rapid departure under Mountbatten and Hindu-Muslim tensions mobilized by the Muslim League and Congress. AP Period 8 tests Partition under GOV and SIO as a traumatic decolonization case and its legacy for India-Pakistan nuclear rivalry.

questions.prompt: The process of colonies gaining independence from imperial powers.
questions.explanation: Decolonization describes the post-WWII wave of European colonial withdrawal and emergence of ~80 new nation-states—primarily in Asia (1945–1955) and Africa (1957–1975). Driven by WWII's weakening of European powers, nationalist movements, Cold War pressure, and UN principles of self-determination. AP Period 8 tests decolonization under GOV as a global transformation reshaping the international order, with varied patterns of negotiated vs. violent independence.

questions.prompt: End of British colonial rule in South Asia, achieved in 1947 after nationalist struggle.
questions.explanation: India achieved independence on August 15, 1947 after decades of nationalist struggle led by the Indian National Congress, Mahatma Gandhi's nonviolent civil disobedience campaigns (Salt March, 1930), and Jawaharlal Nehru's leadership. WWII's financial drain on Britain accelerated the timeline. AP Period 8 tests Indian independence under GOV as a major decolonization case—and as an example of nonviolent resistance as a political strategy with global influence on later civil rights movements.

questions.prompt: The division of British India into India and Pakistan.
questions.explanation: The Partition of British India (August 1947) into India and Pakistan was shaped by Muhammad Ali Jinnah's two-nation theory—that Hindus and Muslims constituted separate nations. The hastily drawn Radcliffe Line created disputed territories, most consequentially Kashmir—still contested today. AP tests it under GOV and SIO as a decolonization case study where religious identity drove political boundaries, producing lasting regional instability.

questions.prompt: Movement from colonies or former colonies to imperial or core cities.
questions.explanation: Post-WWII migration from former colonies to European metropolitan centers—West Indians and South Asians to Britain (Windrush generation, 1948–1971), North Africans to France, Indonesians to the Netherlands—created multicultural societies and new racial tensions. These migrants filled labor shortages in rebuilding economies. AP Period 8 tests migration to metropoles under SIO and CDI as a consequence of decolonization that transformed European societies and challenged European racial identity.

questions.prompt: The policy to stop the spread of communism.
questions.explanation: Containment—articulated by diplomat George Kennan in his 1946 Long Telegram and the 1947 'X Article'—became U.S. Cold War policy under Truman: prevent Soviet expansion without direct military confrontation. The Truman Doctrine (1947), Marshall Plan (1948), and NATO (1949) were early implementations. AP Period 8 tests containment under GOV as the strategic framework explaining U.S. interventions from Greece to Korea to Vietnam—and ultimately the decision to fund proxy wars rather than risk nuclear escalation.

questions.prompt: Revolution that seeks to replace capitalism with one-party socialist or communist rule.
questions.explanation: Post-WWII communist revolutions in China (1949), Cuba (1959), and Vietnam (1975) transformed the Cold War landscape, demonstrating that communism could spread through indigenous revolutionary movements rather than Soviet expansion. Each followed Leninist vanguard party models adapted to peasant societies. AP Period 8 tests communist revolutions under GOV—asking students to analyze causes (colonial grievances, peasant poverty, nationalist appeal) and Cold War superpowers' responses.

questions.prompt: Mao Zedong won this 1949 revolution and founded the People's Republic of China.
questions.explanation: Mao Zedong's Communist Party of China (CPC) defeated the Nationalist Guomindang (GMD) in the Chinese Civil War, founding the People's Republic of China on October 1, 1949. The GMD retreated to Taiwan. Mao's victory—based on peasant mobilization and guerrilla warfare—alarmed the West and intensified Cold War competition in Asia. AP Period 8 tests it under GOV as a major Cold War turning point examining how the 'loss of China' shaped U.S. policy.

questions.prompt: Cold War conflict on the Korean peninsula.
questions.explanation: The Korean War (1950–1953) began when North Korea invaded South Korea (June 25, 1950), prompting a U.S.-led UN coalition intervention. China entered when UN forces approached the Yalu River. The armistice (July 27, 1953) restored roughly the original border at the 38th parallel—a stalemate. AP Period 8 tests the Korean War under GOV as the first direct Cold War military conflict and a test of containment, illustrating how the superpowers fought proxy wars to avoid direct nuclear confrontation.

questions.prompt: Cold War and decolonization conflict in Southeast Asia.
questions.explanation: The Vietnam War (1955–1975) pitted North Vietnam (communist, supported by the USSR and China) against South Vietnam (U.S.-backed). U.S. involvement escalated from advisors to 500,000 troops (1969). The war ended with North Vietnamese victory and reunification (1975). AP Period 8 tests it under GOV as a Cold War proxy conflict shaped by decolonization—Vietnam first fought French colonialism, then Cold War containment—and SIO for its divisive domestic effects in the U.S.

questions.prompt: The policy of not formally siding with either Cold War bloc.
questions.explanation: Nonalignment was a Cold War foreign policy adopted by newly independent states—led by India's Nehru, Yugoslavia's Tito, Egypt's Nasser, and Indonesia's Sukarno—rejecting formal alignment with either the U.S. or Soviet blocs. The Non-Aligned Movement (NAM) formalized at Bandung (1955) and Belgrade (1961). AP Period 8 tests nonalignment under GOV as a Third World strategy for maximizing sovereignty and economic aid from both superpowers simultaneously.

questions.prompt: The policy of not formally siding with either Cold War bloc.
questions.explanation: Nonalignment was a Cold War foreign policy adopted by newly independent states—led by India's Nehru, Yugoslavia's Tito, Egypt's Nasser, and Indonesia's Sukarno—rejecting formal alignment with either the U.S. or Soviet blocs. The Non-Aligned Movement (NAM) formalized at Bandung (1955) and Belgrade (1961). AP Period 8 tests nonalignment under GOV as a Third World strategy for maximizing sovereignty and economic aid from both superpowers simultaneously.

questions.prompt: The movement emphasizing unity among African peoples.
questions.explanation: Pan-Africanism, championed by W.E.B. Du Bois, Marcus Garvey, and later Kwame Nkrumah (Ghana's first president), sought political and cultural unity among African peoples worldwide as a response to colonialism and racism. The Organization of African Unity (OAU, 1963) institutionalized the movement. AP Period 8 tests Pan-Africanism under CDI and GOV as an anti-colonial ideology that shaped decolonization and the formation of new African states.

questions.prompt: The war for independence from France.
questions.explanation: The Algerian War (1954–1962) was a violent independence struggle by the FLN (National Liberation Front) against French colonial rule. France deployed 500,000 troops and used systematic torture; the conflict killed ~500,000. De Gaulle ultimately granted independence (Évian Accords, 1962). AP Period 8 tests it under GOV as an example of violent decolonization from a settler colony—contrasted with Ghana's negotiated independence (1957) as different decolonization patterns.

questions.prompt: Indirect economic or political control over newly independent states.
questions.explanation: Neocolonialism describes the indirect economic and political control exercised by wealthy states over formally independent former colonies—through debt, trade agreements, multinational corporations, and IMF structural adjustment programs requiring privatization and austerity. Kwame Nkrumah coined the term. AP Period 8–9 tests neocolonialism under ECN and GOV as a continuity between formal colonialism and post-colonial dependency, underpinning debates about Global North-South inequality.

questions.prompt: This organization was founded in 1945, replaced the League of Nations, and has its headquarters in New York.
questions.explanation: The United Nations, founded October 24, 1945 with 51 members (now 193), replaced the failed League of Nations with a Security Council (5 permanent veto-holding members: U.S., USSR/Russia, UK, France, China) and a General Assembly. It oversaw decolonization, adopted the Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948), and deployed peacekeeping forces. AP Period 8–9 tests the UN under GOV as a multilateral institution shaped by but often constrained by superpower rivalry.

questions.prompt: Agricultural modernization using high-yield seeds, fertilizer, irrigation, and new technology.
questions.explanation: The Green Revolution (1940s–1970s) introduced high-yield hybrid wheat and rice varieties (Norman Borlaug's work), synthetic fertilizers, pesticides, and irrigation to developing world agriculture—tripling grain yields in Mexico, India, and the Philippines. It prevented mass famines but increased dependence on chemical inputs and water, widening inequality between large and small farmers. AP Period 8 tests it under TEC and ENV as a technological solution to food security with complex social and environmental consequences.

questions.prompt: 1959 revolution that overthrew Batista and brought Fidel Castro to power.
questions.explanation: Fidel Castro's 26th of July Movement overthrew Batista's U.S.-backed regime on January 1, 1959, establishing a communist government 90 miles from Florida. The revolution nationalized U.S. businesses, triggering the U.S. trade embargo (1960) and Bay of Pigs invasion (1961), and led to the Cuban Missile Crisis (1962). AP Period 8 tests the Cuban Revolution under GOV as a Cold War case study in revolutionary nationalism and U.S. interventionism in Latin America.

questions.prompt: Conflicts where major powers support opposing sides instead of fighting directly.
questions.explanation: Proxy wars were conflicts where the U.S. and USSR supported opposing sides without direct combat—Korea, Vietnam, Angola, Afghanistan, Nicaragua. They served Cold War competition while avoiding nuclear escalation between the superpowers. AP Period 8 tests proxy wars under GOV as the primary mechanism of Cold War military competition, illustrating how superpower ideology and interests shaped regional conflicts in the decolonizing world.

questions.prompt: Cold War easing of tensions between rival powers.
questions.explanation: Détente (c. 1969–1979) eased Cold War tensions through negotiated agreements: SALT I (1972) limited nuclear missiles; Nixon's 1972 visit to China normalized U.S.-China relations; the Helsinki Accords (1975) recognized European borders and committed signatories to human rights. AP Period 8 tests détente under GOV as evidence that Cold War competition had limits and that pragmatic diplomacy could reduce existential risk, setting up the question of why it collapsed after the Soviet invasion of Afghanistan (1979).

questions.prompt: Policy ideology favoring deregulation, privatization, free markets, and reduced state intervention.
questions.explanation: Neoliberalism—associated with Reagan, Thatcher, and the 'Washington Consensus'—advocated deregulation, privatization, free trade, and reduced social spending as prescriptions for economic growth. The IMF and World Bank required 'structural adjustment' (austerity) from debtor nations. AP Period 9 tests neoliberalism under ECN and GOV as the dominant economic ideology reshaping global trade from the 1980s onward, and as a contested framework generating inequality alongside growth.

questions.prompt: Moving jobs or production to another country.
questions.explanation: Outsourcing—relocating manufacturing or services to countries with cheaper labor (China, India, Bangladesh, Mexico)—accelerated with containerization and trade liberalization from the 1980s onward, producing 'global factories' where different parts of a product are made in multiple countries. AP Period 9 tests outsourcing under ECN and SIO as a consequence of neoliberal globalization that created growth in developing nations while generating deindustrialization and wage stagnation in wealthy countries.

questions.prompt: International organization created after World War II.
questions.explanation: The UN (founded 1945, now 193 members) coordinates international responses to conflict, development, and humanitarian crises through Security Council enforcement, peacekeeping missions, and specialized agencies (WHO, UNESCO, UNICEF). AP Period 8–9 tests the UN under GOV as the primary multilateral institution of the post-WWII order—evaluating both its successes (smallpox eradication, peacekeeping) and failures (Rwanda, Srebrenica) in enforcing international norms.

questions.prompt: Political violence targeting civilians to create fear and pressure governments.
questions.explanation: Post-Cold War terrorism—especially jihadist movements like Al-Qaeda (9/11 attacks, September 11, 2001) and ISIS—became a defining security challenge of the 21st century, triggering U.S.-led wars in Afghanistan (2001) and Iraq (2003) and reshaping international law, immigration policy, and surveillance. AP Period 9 tests terrorism under GOV as a non-state political violence strategy enabled by globalization's connectivity and as a driver of 21st-century geopolitical conflict.

questions.prompt: UN military or observer missions meant to reduce conflict and protect civilians.
questions.explanation: UN peacekeeping missions deploy multinational military and civilian personnel under Security Council mandate to monitor ceasefires, protect civilians, and support political transitions—from UNTSO (1948) to current operations. Successes include Namibia, Cambodia, and Sierra Leone; failures include Rwanda (1994, ~800,000 killed) and Srebrenica (1995). AP Period 9 tests peacekeeping under GOV as evidence of multilateral governance's reach and limits in the post-Cold War world.

questions.prompt: Standardized cargo system that lowered transport costs and accelerated globalization.
questions.explanation: Standardized shipping containers—pioneered by Malcom McLean (1956)—revolutionized global trade by enabling seamless transfer between ships, trains, and trucks, reducing loading costs by ~90%. By the 1980s containerization had integrated global manufacturing supply chains. AP Period 9 tests container shipping under TEC and ECN as a foundational infrastructure enabling economic globalization—cheaper shipping made global production networks economically viable.

questions.prompt: Trade agreement linking the United States, Canada, and Mexico.
questions.explanation: NAFTA (North American Free Trade Agreement, effective January 1994) eliminated most tariffs between the U.S., Canada, and Mexico, integrating North American manufacturing supply chains. It accelerated Mexican manufacturing (maquiladoras) but displaced U.S. manufacturing jobs and undermined Mexican corn farmers (via cheaper U.S. corn). AP Period 9 tests NAFTA under ECN as a case study in trade liberalization's uneven distributional effects—winners and losers within as well as between countries.

questions.prompt: Global institution that lends money and influences economic policy during financial crises.
questions.explanation: The IMF (founded at Bretton Woods, 1944) provides emergency loans to countries in financial crisis in exchange for 'structural adjustment' conditions—typically requiring austerity, privatization, and trade liberalization. Critics argue these policies worsen poverty; defenders say they stabilize currencies. AP Period 9 tests the IMF under ECN and GOV as a key institution of neoliberal globalization whose conditionality policies shaped developing-world economic policy for decades.

questions.prompt: Technology that accelerated communication and exchange.
questions.explanation: The Internet—evolved from ARPANET (1969) into the World Wide Web (Tim Berners-Lee, 1991) and commercialized through the 1990s—created instantaneous global communication, e-commerce, and information exchange. By 2020, ~4.5 billion people were online. AP Period 9 tests the Internet under TEC and ECN as the enabling infrastructure of digital-age globalization, and CDI for how it accelerates cultural diffusion while enabling surveillance and misinformation.

questions.prompt: The organization overseeing global trade rules.
questions.explanation: The World Trade Organization (WTO, established 1995) replaced GATT and created a binding dispute-resolution mechanism for international trade conflicts—lowering tariff and non-tariff barriers globally. China's 2001 WTO accession accelerated its export-led growth. AP Period 9 tests the WTO under ECN and GOV as the institutional framework of neoliberal trade liberalization, examining arguments that it privileges wealthy-nation interests over developing-country needs.

questions.prompt: Long-term shifts in climate linked to human activity.
questions.explanation: Human-caused climate change—driven by CO2 and methane emissions from fossil fuels and deforestation since industrialization—is projected to raise global temperatures 1.5–4°C by 2100, causing sea-level rise, extreme weather, and mass ecosystem disruption. The IPCC (1988) and Paris Agreement (2015) are key international responses. AP Period 9 tests climate change under ENV and TEC as the defining long-term consequence of industrialization-era fossil fuel use.

questions.prompt: The movement seeking women's political, social, and economic rights.
questions.explanation: Second-wave feminism (1960s–1980s) moved beyond suffrage to demand equal pay, reproductive rights, and freedom from discrimination—producing the Equal Pay Act (U.S., 1963), Roe v. Wade (1973), and UN Convention on the Elimination of All Forms of Discrimination Against Women (CEDAW, 1979). Third-wave feminism (1990s+) expanded intersectionality. AP Period 9 tests feminism under CDI and SIO as a global rights movement reshaping gender hierarchies and state law.

questions.prompt: Agricultural changes that increased food production.
questions.explanation: The Green Revolution introduced high-yield hybrid crop varieties, synthetic fertilizers, and irrigation to the developing world (1940s–1970s), tripling grain production and averting predicted famines. India's wheat production doubled 1965–1972. But it increased chemical inputs, water use, and inequality between large and smallholder farmers. AP Period 9 tests it under TEC and ENV as an example of technological intervention in food security with complex social and environmental trade-offs.

questions.prompt: Movement focused on protecting natural systems from pollution and overuse.
questions.explanation: Modern environmentalism emerged after Rachel Carson's Silent Spring (1962) exposed pesticide harm and gained global momentum through the first Earth Day (1970), the creation of the EPA, and the UN Stockholm Conference (1972). The movement produced international agreements—Montreal Protocol (1987), Kyoto Protocol (1997), Paris Agreement (2015). AP Period 9 tests environmentalism under ENV and CDI as a transnational social movement responding to industrialization's ecological consequences.

questions.prompt: Basic rights claimed for all people.
questions.explanation: The Universal Declaration of Human Rights (UDHR), adopted by the UN General Assembly December 10, 1948, established a global framework of civil, political, economic, social, and cultural rights for all people. Post-WWII Holocaust awareness drove its creation. AP Period 9 tests human rights under CDI and GOV as a cross-border normative framework that challenged state sovereignty and created new international accountability mechanisms, connecting to decolonization, feminist, and LGBTQ+ rights movements.

questions.prompt: Growing connection of economies, cultures, people, and ideas across the world.
questions.explanation: Globalization describes the intensification of economic, cultural, political, and demographic connections across the world—accelerated by containerization, internet, and trade liberalization from the 1970s onward. It increased global GDP but also inequalities within nations. AP Period 9 tests globalization under ECN, CDI, and SIO as the defining process of the late 20th–early 21st century, asking students to evaluate both its homogenizing effects and resistance to it.

questions.prompt: Social pattern in which identity and daily life are shaped by buying goods and services.
questions.explanation: Consumer culture—driven by mass production, advertising, and rising incomes in the post-WWII U.S. and later globally—made identity construction through purchasing commodities a defining social practice. McDonald's, Coca-Cola, and American pop culture spread globally via media and marketing. AP Period 9 tests consumer culture under CDI and ECN as both a form of American 'soft power' exporting Western values and as a contested cultural force generating anti-Western backlash.

questions.prompt: Large movement of people due to labor demand, empire, and economic change.
questions.explanation: Post-WWII global migration intensified with decolonization, economic inequality, and conflict—producing major flows from the Global South to North America and Europe. By 2020, ~280 million international migrants lived outside their birth countries. AP Period 9 tests global migration under SIO and ECN as evidence of globalization's human dimension—analyzing push factors (poverty, conflict, climate) and pull factors (labor demand, higher wages) shaping migration patterns.

questions.prompt: Late twentieth-century shift created by computers, networks, and digital communication.
questions.explanation: The digital revolution (c. 1970s–present) transformed economies, communication, and culture through personal computers, mobile phones, the Internet, and artificial intelligence—shifting from analog/mechanical systems to digital data processing. It created new industries (tech sector), disrupted old ones (print media, retail), and enabled gig economies. AP Period 9 tests it under TEC and ECN as the defining technological shift of the late 20th–21st centuries, analogous to the Industrial Revolution in its scope.

questions.prompt: Newly independent countries that emerged after the collapse of colonial empires.
questions.explanation: The roughly 80 new states that emerged from decolonization (1945–1975) inherited colonial borders, administrative structures, and economies oriented toward commodity export—generating persistent fragility. Many suffered coups, civil wars, and dependency. AP Period 8–9 tests post-colonial states under GOV and ECN through concepts like neocolonialism, structural adjustment, and the legacy of colonial economic extraction—a key theme in AP's examination of continuity vs. change across the modern world.

questions.prompt: Organization of oil-exporting countries that coordinates petroleum policy and can influence prices.
questions.explanation: OPEC (founded 1960) coordinated petroleum policy among member states—initially to counter price-setting by major Western oil companies. The 1973 Arab oil embargo (responding to U.S. support for Israel in the Yom Kippur War) quadrupled oil prices, triggering global recession and reshaping energy policy. AP Period 9 tests OPEC under ECN and GOV as evidence of post-colonial states using resource nationalism to challenge Western economic dominance.

questions.prompt: Global health crisis requiring international response.
questions.explanation: HIV/AIDS, first identified in 1981, had infected ~75 million people and killed ~32 million by 2020—disproportionately in sub-Saharan Africa (where 70% of infections are concentrated). The epidemic exposed global health inequities: antiretroviral therapy was available in wealthy nations years before reaching Africa. AP Period 9 tests HIV/AIDS under ENV and SIO as a global health crisis revealing how disease, poverty, and governance interact, and as a case study in international health diplomacy and NGO activism.

questions.prompt: Small loans used to support entrepreneurs in developing areas.
questions.explanation: Microfinance provides small loans to entrepreneurs in developing nations who lack collateral for conventional bank credit—pioneered by Muhammad Yunus's Grameen Bank in Bangladesh (1983). It targeted women especially. AP Period 9 tests microfinance under ECN and SIO as a poverty-reduction strategy that challenges traditional development models, while critics note high interest rates and mixed evidence of impact on sustained poverty reduction.

questions.prompt: Political and economic organization of many European countries.
questions.explanation: The European Union evolved from the 1957 Treaty of Rome's Common Market through the 1992 Maastricht Treaty—creating a single market with free movement of goods, capital, services, and people, and a common currency (euro, 1999) for most members. It is the world's most advanced regional integration project. AP Period 9 tests the EU under GOV and ECN as a case study in supranational governance challenging traditional state sovereignty and as a model of regional economic integration.

questions.prompt: Disease outbreak spreading across countries or continents with major social and economic effects.
questions.explanation: Globalization's intensified human movement accelerated disease transmission: SARS (2003), H1N1 influenza (2009), Ebola (2014–16), and COVID-19 (2019–present, ~7 million official deaths) illustrate how rapidly pathogens cross borders. The WHO coordinates international response but national sovereignty complicates coordination. AP Period 9 tests pandemic disease under ENV and ECN as a 21st-century consequence of globalization—connecting to Period 2's Black Death as a recurring AP comparison prompt.

questions.prompt: East Asian state known for bureaucracy, Confucianism, commercialization, and technology.
questions.explanation: The Song dynasty (960–1279) pioneered paper money, gunpowder weapons, and the civil service exam (keju), making it one of history's most commercially and technologically innovative states before the Mongol conquest. Its population reached ~100 million by 1100, fueled by Champa rice and commercialization. AP World uses Song China across Periods 1–2 under ECN, TEC, and GOV as the paradigm of East Asian state sophistication.

questions.prompt: Conquest and settlement in the Americas centered on extraction and conversion.
questions.explanation: Spanish colonization (c. 1492–1820s) combined military conquest (Cortés defeated the Aztec 1521, Pizarro the Inca 1532), forced conversion, silver extraction, and encomienda/mita labor systems. It created the casta racial hierarchy and destroyed pre-Columbian states. AP Period 4 tests Spanish colonization under GOV, SIO, and ECN—frequently in comparison with Portuguese, Dutch, or British colonial models.

questions.prompt: Global conflict from 1914 to 1918.
questions.explanation: WWI (1914–1918) mobilized the industrial and colonial resources of empires across six continents, killing ~20 million and transforming politics, economics, and culture. Machine guns, poison gas, and artillery produced unprecedented trench stalemate on the Western Front. AP Period 7 tests WWI under GOV and TEC as the catastrophe that shattered the 19th-century order and set conditions—via the punitive Versailles settlement—for WWII.

questions.prompt: Chinese belief system emphasizing hierarchy, filial piety, and social order.
questions.explanation: Confucianism—originating with Confucius (551–479 BCE)—structured East Asian governance, social hierarchy, and family relations around filial piety, ritual propriety, and meritocratic bureaucracy for over two millennia. Revived as Song Neo-Confucianism, it spread to Korea, Japan, and Vietnam. AP World tests Confucianism under CDI and GOV across Periods 1–3 as the dominant legitimizing ideology of Chinese imperial states.

questions.prompt: Peace settlement whose punishments helped destabilize interwar Europe.
questions.explanation: The Treaty of Versailles (June 28, 1919) imposed on Germany sole war guilt, 132 billion gold marks in reparations, territorial losses, and military restrictions. The humiliation and economic burden fueled Weimar instability and Hitler's rise. AP Period 7 tests it under GOV as a failed peace settlement—the classic 'punitive peace creates future war' argument—and as evidence of how post-WWI power politics undermined Wilsonian self-determination ideals.

questions.prompt: Overland trade routes linking East Asia, Central Asia, the Middle East, and Europe.
questions.explanation: The Silk Roads—a network of overland and maritime routes from China to the Mediterranean active c. 200 BCE–1450 CE—moved luxury goods but more consequentially spread religions (Buddhism, Islam, Christianity), technologies (paper, stirrup, gunpowder), and diseases (Black Death). AP Period 2 tests the Silk Roads under ECN and CDI as the core Eurasian exchange network and the paradigm case for how long-distance trade drives cultural transformation.

questions.prompt: Trading-post empire built through navigation, forts, and sea power.
questions.explanation: Portugal's Estado da India (early 1500s onward) used fortified feitorias and naval dominance rather than territorial conquest to control key nodes—Hormuz, Goa, Malacca, Macao—in the Indian Ocean and Asian trade network. This trading-post model was more ECN than GOV-driven and differed fundamentally from Spanish settler colonialism. AP Period 4 tests it as the first European maritime empire and the template copied by Dutch and English competitors.

questions.prompt: Maritime trade network connecting East Africa, Arabia, South Asia, and Southeast Asia.
questions.explanation: The Indian Ocean trade network, powered by predictable monsoon winds, connected East Africa, Arabia, Persia, India, and Southeast Asia in a web of commerce exchanging spices, textiles, gold, and porcelain. Muslim, Indian, and Chinese merchants dominated different segments. AP World tests it across Periods 1–4 under ECN and CDI—it is one of the two most important exchange networks in AP World (alongside the Silk Roads).

questions.prompt: Fast-ripening rice that supported population growth in China.
questions.explanation: Champa rice, introduced from the Champa kingdom to Song China c. 1012 CE, could be harvested twice annually on marginal land, enabling China's population to roughly double to ~100 million by 1100. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled urbanization and commercialization. AP World Period 1 tests Champa rice under ENV and ECN as an example of agricultural technology transfer driving demographic and economic transformation.

questions.prompt: Trade network linking West Africa and North Africa across the Sahara.
questions.explanation: Camel caravans crossing the Sahara since ~300 CE linked West African gold (Bambuk, Bure) with North African salt and Mediterranean markets, enriching Mali and Songhai empires and spreading Islam across the region. The trade is a key ECN and CDI example in AP Period 1–2, linking West Africa into the broader Afro-Eurasian exchange system and explaining Islam's rapid diffusion south of the Sahara.

questions.prompt: Ottoman system recruiting Christian boys for military and bureaucratic service.
questions.explanation: Devshirme ('collection') was the Ottoman practice of levying Christian boys from Balkan subjects, converting them to Islam, and training them as elite soldiers (janissaries) or administrators. It built a loyal imperial service outside the hereditary aristocracy. AP Period 3 tests devshirme under GOV and SIO as an innovative strategy for creating a meritocratic bureaucratic-military corps loyal to the sultan rather than feudal landholders.

questions.prompt: Intellectual movement emphasizing reason, natural rights, and reform.
questions.explanation: The Enlightenment (c. 1680–1789) argued that reason, empirical observation, and natural law should replace tradition and divine authority in governance and society. Locke, Rousseau, Montesquieu, and Voltaire challenged monarchy, slavery, and religious intolerance. AP Period 5 tests the Enlightenment under CDI and GOV as the intellectual engine driving Atlantic revolutions and liberal constitutionalism—a thread continuing through decolonization and human rights movements.

questions.prompt: Thinker associated with natural rights and consent of the governed.
questions.explanation: John Locke (1632–1704) argued government must be based on consent of the governed to protect natural rights—life, liberty, property. His Two Treatises of Government (1689) directly shaped Jefferson's Declaration of Independence and the U.S. Constitution. AP Period 5 tests Locke under CDI as the intellectual foundation for constitutional government and the philosophical legitimization of revolution against tyranny.

questions.prompt: Transfer of plants, animals, people, diseases, and ideas between hemispheres.
questions.explanation: The Columbian Exchange—Alfred Crosby's term for the bi-directional biological transfer between hemispheres after 1492—was perhaps history's most consequential ecological event: New World crops (maize, potatoes) boosted Old World populations from China to Europe; Old World diseases (smallpox, measles) killed ~50–90% of Indigenous American populations. AP Period 4 tests it under ECN, ENV, and CDI as the biological foundation of Atlantic colonialism.

questions.prompt: Colonial rebellion that created the United States.
questions.explanation: The American Revolution (1775–1783) established the first written national constitution (1787) and a republic grounded in Enlightenment principles—consent of the governed, separation of powers, natural rights. It inspired subsequent Atlantic revolutions but preserved slavery and excluded women and Indigenous peoples from citizenship. AP Period 5 tests it under GOV as a political revolution that changed governance while perpetuating social hierarchies.

questions.prompt: The policy of extending control over other territories or peoples.
questions.explanation: The New Imperialism (1870s–1914) saw industrial European powers colonize 84% of Earth's surface, driven by demands for raw materials, markets, and strategic positioning—justified ideologically by Social Darwinism and the 'civilizing mission.' AP Period 6 tests imperialism under GOV, ECN, and SIO as the defining political-economic process of the late 19th century, generating the racial hierarchies and economic dependencies that shaped 20th-century decolonization.

questions.prompt: Militarism, alliances, imperialism, and nationalism as causes of World War I.
questions.explanation: The MAIN framework identifies structural causes of WWI: Militarism (arms race, glorification of war), Alliances (Triple Entente vs. Triple Alliance creating chain reactions), Imperialism (colonial rivalry in Africa and Asia generating European tensions), and Nationalism (ethnic self-determination challenges in Austria-Hungary and the Balkans). The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand (June 28, 1914) triggered these structural conditions. AP Period 7 uses MAIN under GOV for causation analysis.

questions.prompt: The misused idea claiming stronger nations or groups were naturally superior.
questions.explanation: Social Darwinism misapplied Darwin's natural selection to human societies, claiming 'superior' races or nations naturally dominated 'inferior' ones—used to justify imperial conquest, racial segregation, and genocide. Herbert Spencer coined 'survival of the fittest.' AP Period 6 tests Social Darwinism under CDI and GOV as the pseudoscientific ideology legitimizing late 19th-century imperialism, alongside Christian missionary 'civilizing mission' justifications.

questions.prompt: The meeting where European powers divided Africa without African consent.
questions.explanation: The Berlin Conference (1884–1885) formalized European partition of Africa—establishing 'effective occupation' as the standard for colonial claims—without any African representation. It accelerated the Scramble for Africa: from ~10% to ~90% European control by 1914. AP Period 6 tests it under GOV and SIO as the moment European powers institutionalized African partition, creating colonial borders whose arbitrary ethnic composition still generates conflict.

questions.prompt: International organization created after World War II.
questions.explanation: The United Nations (founded October 1945) replaced the failed League of Nations with stronger dispute mechanisms—a Security Council with five permanent veto-holding members and peacekeeping forces. It oversaw decolonization, adopted the UDHR (1948), and became the primary venue for multilateral diplomacy. AP Period 8–9 tests the UN under GOV as evidence of post-WWII institutional multilateralism—evaluating its successes and structural limitations shaped by superpower veto power.

questions.prompt: Muslim empire controlling Anatolia, southeastern Europe, and the eastern Mediterranean.
questions.explanation: At its peak under Suleiman I (r. 1520–1566), the Ottoman Empire controlled Anatolia, the Balkans, the Levant, Egypt, and North Africa—the largest Muslim empire since the Abbasids. It fell gradually from 1699 (Treaty of Karlowitz) to 1922 (abolition of the sultanate). AP Period 3–7 tests the Ottomans under GOV across multiple centuries—from gunpowder empire model to 'Sick Man of Europe' whose dismemberment contributed to WWI via the mandate system.

questions.prompt: Ideological rivalry between the United States and Soviet Union.
questions.explanation: The Cold War (c. 1947–1991) organized global politics into two blocs—U.S.-led capitalism and Soviet-led communism—competing through arms races, proxy wars, and ideological competition without direct military confrontation. It shaped decolonization (superpowers backed rival independence movements), economic development (competing aid packages), and cultural production globally. AP Period 8 tests the Cold War under GOV and CDI as the defining geopolitical framework of the second half of the 20th century.

questions.prompt: Growing connection of economies, cultures, people, and ideas across the world.
questions.explanation: Globalization—the intensification of economic, cultural, political, and demographic connections across the world—accelerated after the Cold War through trade liberalization, digital technology, and containerized shipping. It increased global GDP but also within-country inequality. AP Period 9 tests globalization under ECN, CDI, and SIO as the defining 21st-century process, asking students to analyze whether it represents a new era or a continuation of earlier exchange network patterns.

questions.prompt: Persian empire that promoted Shia Islam.
questions.explanation: The Safavid Empire (1501–1722) made Twelver Shia Islam Iran's state religion, creating a distinct Persian-Islamic identity in tension with Sunni Ottomans—who fought multiple wars for Anatolia and Mesopotamia (Chaldiran 1514, etc.). Shah Abbas I's capital Isfahan was a showcase of Persian art and architecture. AP Period 3 tests the Safavids under GOV and CDI as a gunpowder empire using sectarian identity as a political tool.

questions.prompt: The policy to stop the spread of communism.
questions.explanation: Containment—Kennan's 1946–1947 strategy—held that Soviet expansion should be resisted wherever it occurred, without seeking to 'roll back' communism. It operationalized through the Truman Doctrine (1947, aid to Greece/Turkey), Marshall Plan (1948), NATO (1949), and military interventions in Korea and Vietnam. AP Period 8 tests containment under GOV as the master U.S. Cold War strategy explaining two major wars and dozens of covert interventions spanning four decades.
